mappings.rationaleAI: Confucianism from its sixth-century BCE formulation as part of the Classical period of Chinese philosophy alongside Taoism and the 'hundred schools' (B.2.e). Covers Confucianism's influence on state ideology, imperial government, and civil service examinations in the Han period (C.2.c.iii) and its subsequent relationship with Buddhism during the Six Dynasties (D.1.b). Impact on Tang cultural and institutional life (D.2.a).

Chinese literary culture from antiquity encompassing poetry, narrative, and philosophical foundations including Confucian and Daoist traditions -- the Classical period of Chinese literature (B.2.e). Covers classical historical records and literati traditions relevant to Han cultural life and prose writing (C.2.c.iii). Literary developments through the Tang dynasty (D.2.a).

Buddhism's introduction to China during the Han period (C.2.c.iii) and its spread as a major intellectual and religious trend during the Six Dynasties period, when Confucianism declined and Buddhism expanded significantly (D.1.b).

Central Eurasian trade and cultural exchange covering Han dynasty relations with other peoples and Pan Ch'ao's campaigns in Central Asia (C.2.c.ii). Silk Road commerce and cultural transmission during the Sui and Tang dynasties (D.2.a).
History of writing systems including Chinese characters as a logographic system, relevant to the development of script during the Shang period at Anyang (B.2.b).

Archaeology as a scientific discipline reconstructing human pasts through excavation and laboratory techniques -- relevant to archaeological sources for Chinese civilization (A). Covers prehistoric periods including Paleolithic and Mesolithic industries (B.1.a), Neolithic settlements (B.1.b), Yang-shao Painted Pottery culture (B.1.c), and Lung-shan Black Pottery complex (B.1.d).

Dynastic rule covering succession, governance, and family-based political legitimacy across Chinese dynasties -- the early dynasties (B.2), Ch'in and Han unification (C, C.1, C.2), and reunification under Sui and Tang (D.2).

Modern China from the late imperial period providing broad context for understanding the character and achievements of Chinese civilization and its continuity from ancient foundations to the present (A).

The Mongol Empire's military campaigns and governance strategies, relevant to the era of barbarian invasions and the period of the Sixteen Kingdoms that characterized the division of the empire during the Six Dynasties (D.1.a).

Prehistory spanning from earliest human ancestors to the beginning of written records, covering the Paleolithic and Mesolithic stages (B.1.a) and Neolithic settlement with the invention of agriculture and domesticated animals (B.1.b). Chinese and central Asian evidence reframing traditional Eurasian narratives (B.1).
